examples.explanation: Start at index 0 (value 2). Try jump 1 to index 1 (value 3). From index 1: try jump 1 to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: try jump 1 to index 3 (value 1). From index 3: try jump 1 to index 4 (last index). Return true. No need to explore other branches.

Start at index 0 (value 3). Try jump 1 to index 1 (value 2). From index 1: try jump 1 to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: try jump 1 to index 3 (value 0). From index 3: maxJump=0, no jumps possible, return false. Backtrack to index 2: try jump 2 to index 4 (exceeds bounds, invalid). Return false. Backtrack to index 1: try jump 2 to index 3 (value 0). From index 3: maxJump=0, return false. Backtrack to index 0: try jump 2 to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: try jump 1 to index 3 (value 0), return false; try jump 2 to index 4 (exceeds bounds). Return false. Try jump 3 from index 0 to index 3 (value 0), return false. All branches exhausted, return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible jumps from each position to explore every path to the end. Use backtracking to check if any sequence of jumps reaches the last index.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Backtracking for Jump Game
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all possible jump sequences to determine if last index is reachable.
ladders.explanation: The brute force backtracking approach solves the Jump Game problem by systematically exploring every possible sequence of jumps from the starting position to see if any path reaches the last index. The intuition is that at each position, we can jump anywhere from 1 to nums[i] steps forward, creating a branching tree of possibilities. We recursively explore each branch until we either reach the last index (success) or exhaust all possibilities (failure). The algorithm starts at index 0 and recursively tries all possible jumps from the current position. For each jump distance from 1 to nums[current], we move to the new position and continue exploring. If we reach the last index, we return true. If all jumps from a position fail, we backtrack to try other paths. The base cases are: reaching last index (true), or being at a position with 0 jump capacity (false). Time complexity is O(2^n) because in worst case (like [n,n,n,...]) each position can branch to n possibilities, creating exponential paths. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack depth. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive exploration that uses the backtracking subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling the base case properly, forgetting to check bounds, or not returning the result correctly from recursive calls. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): def backtrack(pos): if pos >= len(nums)-1: return True; maxJump = nums[pos]; for jump in range(1, maxJump+1): if backtrack(pos+jump): return True; return False; return backtrack(0).

examples.trace: Start at index 0 (value 2). Try jump 1 to index 1 (value 3). From index 1: try jump 1 to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: try jump 1 to index 3 (value 1). From index 3: try jump 1 to index 4 (last index). Return true. No need to explore other branches.

Start at index 0 (value 3). Try jump 1 to index 1 (value 2). From index 1: try jump 1 to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: try jump 1 to index 3 (value 0). From index 3: maxJump=0, no jumps possible, return false. Backtrack to index 2: try jump 2 to index 4 (exceeds bounds, invalid). Return false. Backtrack to index 1: try jump 2 to index 3 (value 0). From index 3: maxJump=0, return false. Backtrack to index 0: try jump 2 to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: try jump 1 to index 3 (value 0), return false; try jump 2 to index 4 (exceeds bounds). Return false. Try jump 3 from index 0 to index 3 (value 0), return false. All branches exhausted, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Backtracking from a Position
ladders.desc: Helper function that recursively explores all jump possibilities from a given index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that implements backtracking from a specific position. The intuition is that to determine if we can reach the end from position 'pos', we need to check all possible jumps we can make from that position. For each valid jump distance (from 1 to nums[pos]), we recursively check if we can reach the end from the new position. If any recursive call returns true, we propagate that success upward. If all jumps fail, we return false. The algorithm: function backtrack(pos): if pos >= last index: return true; maxJump = nums[pos]; for jump from 1 to maxJump: newPos = pos + jump; if backtrack(newPos): return true; return false. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(b^d) where b is branching factor and d is depth, but in context of full problem it contributes to O(2^n). Space is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the main recursive engine that gets called from the starting position. Common mistakes include infinite recursion (not checking bounds), incorrect base case (should be >= not ==), and not returning the recursive result properly. Real code pattern: def backtrack(pos): if pos >= len(nums)-1: return True; for jump in range(1, nums[pos]+1): if backtrack(pos+jump): return True; return False.

examples.trace: At pos=1 (value 3). Check if pos>=4? No. maxJump=3. Try jump=1 to pos=2. Recursively call backtrack(2). At pos=2 (value 1): try jump=1 to pos=3. Recursively call backtrack(3). At pos=3 (value 1): try jump=1 to pos=4. Recursively call backtrack(4). pos=4 >=4, return true. Propagate true upward through all calls.

At pos=2 (value 1). Check if pos>=4? No. maxJump=1. Try jump=1 to pos=3. Recursively call backtrack(3). At pos=3 (value 0): maxJump=0, no jumps, check if pos>=4? No, return false. Back to pos=2: no more jumps, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exploring All Jump Combinations via Recursion
ladders.desc: The recursive pattern that tries every possible jump sequence by branching at each position.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the fundamental recursive exploration pattern. The intuition is that reaching the last index requires making a series of jumps, and at each step we have multiple choices (1 to nums[i] steps). We need to check all possible combinations of these choices. The algorithm uses depth-first search with backtracking: from current position, try each possible jump distance, recursively explore from the new position, and if that path fails, backtrack to try the next jump. The key insight is that this creates a decision tree where each node represents a position, and edges represent jump choices. We traverse this tree until we find a leaf node that's the last index (success) or exhaust all paths (failure). Time complexity arises from the branching factor at each node potentially being up to n, leading to exponential possibilities. Space is linear for the recursion depth. This connects to L1 as the pattern that the backtracking subroutine implements. Common mistakes include not pruning invalid jumps (beyond array bounds), incorrect order of trying jumps, or not properly returning results. Real code pattern: def explore(pos): for jump in possible_jumps: new_pos = pos + jump; result = explore(new_pos); if result: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Position tree: Root (0). Branch jump=1 to node (1) - last index, success. Branch jump=2 to node (2) - out of bounds, invalid. So try jump=1 first, find success, return true without trying jump=2.
Position tree: Root (0). Only jump=1 to node (1). From node (1): nums[1]=0, no jumps possible, not at last index. Return false. Back to root: no more jumps. Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implementing a recursive function that handles termination conditions and makes recursive calls.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to structure a recursive function for decision problems. The intuition is that recursion solves problems by breaking them into smaller subproblems of the same type. For jump exploration, the subproblem is 'can I reach the end from position p?' The base cases handle the simplest scenarios: (1) if already at or beyond last index, return true (success); (2) if at a position with 0 jump capacity, return false (cannot proceed). The recursive case: for each possible jump, make a recursive call to solve the subproblem from the new position. The function returns true if any recursive call returns true, false otherwise. Time complexity depends on recursion tree size. Space is O(recursion depth). This connects to L2 as the implementation of the exploration pattern. Common mistakes include missing base cases, infinite recursion, incorrect combination of results, or stack overflow for deep recursion. Real code pattern: def solve(pos): if base_case_met: return result; for option in options: if solve(new_state): return True; return False.

examples.input: Function to check if can reach index 2 from pos with nums=[1,1,0]
examples.output: true from pos=0, false from pos=2
examples.trace: From pos=0: base case? pos=0 < 2. maxJump=1. Try jump=1 to pos=1. Recursive call from pos=1: base case? pos=1 < 2. maxJump=1. Try jump=1 to pos=2. Recursive call from pos=2: base case? pos=2 >=2, return true. Propagate true back. From pos=2: base case? pos=2 >=2, return true immediately.
From pos=0: base case? pos=0 < 1. maxJump=0. Since maxJump=0 and not at last index, return false (implicit base case).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Possible Jumps from an Index
ladders.desc: Looping through all valid jump distances (1 to nums[i]) from a given array position.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves determining and iterating through all possible jumps from a current position in the array. The intuition is that from index i, you can jump 1, 2, ..., up to nums[i] steps forward, provided you don't exceed array bounds. The operation requires: (1) getting the maximum jump length from current position (nums[i]), (2) generating all jump distances from 1 to that maximum, (3) calculating new positions by adding jump distance to current index, and (4) ensuring new position is within array bounds (though in backtracking we may check bounds in base case). The algorithm: max_jump = nums[current_index]; for jump_distance in range(1, max_jump+1): new_position = current_index + jump_distance; // then process new_position. Time complexity is O(max_jump) for this operation. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as the mechanism for generating options in the recursive case. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (should include max_jump), not handling zero jump capacity, or not checking array bounds. Real code pattern: for jump in range(1, nums[i]+1): next_pos = i + jump.

examples.trace: At index 0, nums[0]=3. Jump distances: 1, 2, 3. New positions: 0+1=1, 0+2=2, 0+3=3. All are within array of length 3 (indices 0-2), but position 3 is out of bounds (will be handled by base case).
At index 0, nums[0]=0. Jump distances from 1 to 0: empty range. No jumps possible.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Value Access
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array by index and understanding index-value relationships.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers basic array operations: accessing an element at a specific index and understanding what the value represents. In the Jump Game problem, the array 'nums' contains integers where nums[i] represents the maximum jump length from position i. The intuition is that arrays are zero-indexed collections where each position i holds a value that provides information about that position. Key operations: (1) Get array length: n = len(nums), (2) Access element at index i: value = nums[i], (3) Understand valid indices range from 0 to n-1. For example, if nums = [2,3,1], then nums[0]=2 means from index 0 you can jump up to 2 steps, nums[1]=3 means from index 1 you can jump up to 3 steps. The last index is n-1 (e.g., for length 3, last index is 2). Time complexity for access is O(1). Space is O(1). This connects to L4 as the prerequisite for getting jump capacity at a position. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, confusing index with value, or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: value = nums[index]; last_index = len(nums)-1.

examples.trace: Array has indices 0,1,2. Index 2 corresponds to the third element. nums[2] = 3. This means from position 2, maximum jump length is 3.

examples.input: nums=[1,2,3,4], find last index and its value
examples.trace: Array length = 4. Last index = length-1 = 3. nums[3] = 4. So from last position, max jump is 4 (though irrelevant since already at end).

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store whether each index can reach the end, avoiding redundant calculations. This top-down approach reduces exponential time to polynomial by caching results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to determine if you can reach the last index.
ladders.explanation: The Jump Game problem asks whether you can reach the last index of an array starting from the first index, where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The dynamic programming with memoization approach solves this by breaking it into overlapping subproblems: from any index i, you can jump to indices i+1 through i+nums[i]. The key insight is that whether you can reach the end from index i depends on whether you can reach the end from any reachable next index. This creates exponential recursion without memoization. By storing results in a memo array (where memo[i] = true/false/unknown), we avoid redundant calculations. The algorithm uses a recursive helper function canReach(i) that returns true if the end is reachable from i. Base cases: if i >= n-1 (at or beyond end), return true; if memo[i] is known, return it. Otherwise, try all possible jumps from 1 to nums[i], recursively calling canReach(i+j). If any returns true, set memo[i] = true and return true; else set memo[i] = false and return false. Start with canReach(0). Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case because each index can try up to n jumps, and memoization ensures each index is computed once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and memo array. This top-down approach is intuitive but less efficient than greedy O(n) solutions, making it excellent for learning DP concepts. Common mistakes include not handling base cases correctly, especially when i exceeds array bounds, or incorrectly initializing memo (use a sentinel like -1 for unknown). The connection to L1 is that this level implements the full recursive structure around the helper function.

examples.trace: Start: canReach(0), n=5, memo=[-1,-1,-1,-1,-1]. i=0, nums[0]=2, try j=1: canReach(1). i=1, nums[1]=3, try j=1: canReach(2). i=2, nums[2]=1, try j=1: canReach(3). i=3, nums[3]=1, try j=1: canReach(4). i=4, base case (i>=4), return true. Backtrack: memo[3]=true, return true. memo[2]=true, return true. memo[1]=true, return true. memo[0]=true, return true. Final: true.

Start: canReach(0), n=5, memo=[-1,-1,-1,-1,-1]. i=0, nums[0]=3, try j=1: canReach(1). i=1, nums[1]=2, try j=1: canReach(2). i=2, nums[2]=1, try j=1: canReach(3). i=3, nums[3]=0, try j=1: i+j=4, canReach(4). i=4, base case, return true. Backtrack: memo[3] tries j=1 only (since nums[3]=0), but i+j=4 returns true, so memo[3]=true, return true. memo[2]=true, return true. Now i=1 continues: try j=2: canReach(3) returns true, so memo[1]=true, return true. i=0 continues: try j=2: canReach(2) returns true, so memo[0]=true, return true. Wait, correction: Actually, from i=3, nums[3]=0 means no jumps possible, so canReach(3) should try j from 1 to 0, which means no recursive calls, and since i=3 < n-1=4, it should return false. Let's re-trace correctly: i=3, nums[3]=0, loop j from 1 to 0 (no iterations), so no true returns, set memo[3]=false, return false. Then i=2: try j=1: canReach(3) returns false, so no true, set memo[2]=false, return false. i=1: try j=1: canReach(2) returns false; try j=2: canReach(3) returns false; set memo[1]=false, return false. i=0: try j=1: canReach(1) returns false; try j=2: canReach(2) returns false; try j=3: canReach(3) returns false; set memo[0]=false, return false. Final: false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that checks reachability from a given index with memoization.
ladders.explanation: The core of the DP solution is the recursive helper function canReach(i), which determines if the last index is reachable starting from index i. The intuition is to treat each index as a subproblem: if we know whether we can reach the end from future indices, we can decide for the current index by trying all possible jumps. Memoization stores results to avoid recomputation. Steps: 1. Base case: if i >= n-1 (where n is array length), return true because we're at or past the end. 2. Check memo[i]: if it's not unknown (e.g., -1), return the stored value (true/false). 3. Otherwise, iterate j from 1 to nums[i] (the maximum jump length). For each j, recursively call canReach(i+j). If any returns true, set memo[i] = true and return true. 4. If no jump leads to true, set memo[i] = false and return false. Time complexity for this function alone is O(n) per call in worst case due to the loop, but with memoization across calls, overall becomes O(n²). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and memo. Connection to L0: this function is called repeatedly in the full solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to check memo before recursion, incorrect loop bounds (j should start at 1, not 0, to avoid infinite recursion), or not handling when nums[i] is 0. Real code pattern: def canReach(i, nums, memo): if i >= len(nums)-1: return True; if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): if canReach(i+j, nums, memo): memo[i] = True; return True; memo[i] = False; return False.

examples.trace: Call canReach(2). n=5, i=2 < 4, memo[2]=-1. nums[2]=1, loop j=1: call canReach(3). i=3, memo[3]=-1, nums[3]=1, loop j=1: call canReach(4). i=4, base case (i>=4), return true. Backtrack: memo[3]=true, return true. memo[2]=true, return true.

Call canReach(1). n=5, i=1 < 4, memo[1]=-1. nums[1]=2, loop j=1: call canReach(2). i=2, memo[2]=-1, nums[2]=1, loop j=1: call canReach(3). i=3, memo[3]=-1, nums[3]=0, loop j from 1 to 0 (none), set memo[3]=false, return false. Backtrack: memo[2]=false, return false. i=1 continues: try j=2: call canReach(3) returns false. No true returns, set memo[1]=false, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Memoization for Overlapping Subproblems
ladders.desc: Understand and implement memoization to cache results of subproblems.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is a technique to store results of expensive function calls and return cached results when the same inputs occur again. In the Jump Game, the subproblem "can we reach the end from index i?" is computed multiple times in naive recursion because different paths may revisit the same index. For example, from index 0, you might jump to index 2 via different routes. Memoization avoids this by using an array memo where memo[i] stores the result for index i (e.g., true, false, or a sentinel like -1 for unknown). The intuition is that once we determine reachability from an index, we never need to recompute it. Steps: 1. Initialize memo with a sentinel (e.g., -1) for all indices, indicating unknown. 2. In the recursive function, before computing, check if memo[i] is known; if so, return it. 3. After computing, store the result in memo[i]. This transforms exponential time O(2^n) to polynomial O(n²). Time analysis: each index's computation involves checking up to n jumps, and with n indices, worst-case O(n²). Space: O(n) for memo array. Connection to L1: this logic is embedded in the helper function. Common mistakes: using a dictionary without handling array bounds, not initializing memo properly, or storing results incorrectly. Real code pattern: memo = [-1] * n; def canReach(i): if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; ... compute result ... memo[i] = result; return result.

examples.trace: Initialize memo=[-1,-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start from i=0: compute, finds true via i=1,2,3,4. Store memo[0]=true. i=1: compute, finds true via i=2,3,4. Store memo[1]=true. i=2: compute, finds true via i=3,4. Store memo[2]=true. i=3: compute, finds true via i=4. Store memo[3]=true. i=4: base case, memo[4]=true. Final memo=[true,true,true,true,true].

Initialize memo=[-1,-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start from i=4: base case, memo[4]=true. i=3: nums[3]=0, no jumps, compute false, memo[3]=false. i=2: try jump to i=3 (false), memo[2]=false. i=1: try jumps to i=2 (false) and i=3 (false), memo[1]=false. i=0: try jumps to i=1,2,3 (all false), memo[0]=false. Final memo=[false,false,false,false,true].

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implement recursion to explore all possible jumps from an index.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a method where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. In Jump Game, from any index i, we need to explore all possible jumps to indices i+1 through i+nums[i]. This is naturally recursive: to check reachability from i, we recursively check from each next index. The intuition is breaking the problem into smaller subproblems: if we can reach the end from any next index, then we can from i. Steps: 1. Define function canReach(i). 2. Base case: if i is at or beyond the last index (i >= n-1), return true. 3. Recursive case: for each j from 1 to nums[i], call canReach(i+j). If any returns true, return true. 4. If none return true, return false. Time complexity without memoization is exponential O(2^n) because each index can branch into up to n calls. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L2: this recursion creates overlapping subproblems that memoization optimizes. Common mistakes: missing base case leading to infinite recursion, incorrect loop bounds (e.g., starting j from 0 causes infinite loop at i), or not handling when nums[i] is 0. Real code pattern: def canReach(i, nums): if i >= len(nums)-1: return True; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): if canReach(i+j, nums): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Call canReach(0). i=0 < 4, nums[0]=2. Try j=1: call canReach(1). i=1 < 4, nums[1]=3. Try j=1: call canReach(2). i=2 < 4, nums[2]=1. Try j=1: call canReach(3). i=3 < 4, nums[3]=1. Try j=1: call canReach(4). i=4 >= 4, return true. Backtrack: canReach(3) returns true, canReach(2) returns true, canReach(1) returns true, canReach(0) returns true.
Call canReach(0). i=0 < 3, nums[0]=1. Try j=1: call canReach(1). i=1 < 3, nums[1]=0, no j loop, return false. Backtrack: canReach(0) returns false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Possible Jumps
ladders.desc: Loop through all possible jump distances from an index.
ladders.explanation: A fundamental operation in Jump Game is iterating over all possible jumps from a current index i. Given nums[i] as the maximum jump length, you can jump to any index from i+1 to i+nums[i], inclusive. This requires a loop that goes through each possible jump distance j. The intuition is to explore all reachable next positions to see if any lead to the end. Steps: 1. For an index i with value nums[i], determine the range of j: from 1 to nums[i] (since jumping 0 places doesn't progress). 2. For each j in this range, compute the next index as i+j. 3. Check if this next index is within array bounds (though in recursion, base cases handle out-of-bounds). Time complexity for this operation is O(nums[i]), which in worst case is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: this loop is inside the recursive function to try each jump. Common mistakes: starting j from 0 (causes infinite recursion or no progress), going beyond nums[i] (e.g., using <= instead of < in range), or not handling when nums[i] is 0 (loop should not execute). Real code pattern: for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): next_index = i + j; ...

examples.trace: i=1, nums[1]=3. Loop j from 1 to 3: j=1 -> next index=2; j=2 -> next index=3; j=3 -> next index=4. So possible jumps to indices 2, 3, and 4.
i=3, nums[3]=0. Loop j from 1 to 0: no iterations, so no possible jumps.

testCases.expected: jumps to indices 1,2,3,4,5 (but array length 1, so only within bounds)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Understand how to access array elements and check indices within bounds.
ladders.explanation: Array indexing is accessing an element in an array using its position (index). In most programming languages, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. For an array of length n, valid indices range from 0 to n-1. Bounds checking ensures we only access indices within this range to avoid errors like index out of bounds. In Jump Game, we frequently access nums[i] to get the jump length and compute i+j for next indices. The intuition is to safely navigate the array without crashing. Steps: 1. Given array nums with length n, to access element at index i, use nums[i]. 2. Check if i is within bounds: 0 <= i < n. 3. When computing i+j, ensure i+j < n to stay within bounds, though in recursion, we may allow i+j >= n and handle it with base cases. Time and space complexity are O(1) for these operations. Connection to L4: this foundation is used when iterating jumps. Common mistakes: assuming 1-indexing, not checking bounds leading to runtime errors, or misunderstanding array length. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); if 0 <= i < n: value = nums[i]; next_index = i + j; if next_index < n: ...

examples.trace: Array length n=5. Index i=2 is between 0 and 4, so valid. Access nums[2] = 1. If computing i+j, e.g., j=1, next_index=3, which is <5, so valid.
Array length n=3. Index i=3 is not <3, so invalid. Accessing nums[3] would cause an error.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Tabulation O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the end to the start, using a boolean array to mark reachable indices. This bottom-up approach builds solutions incrementally without recursion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with DP Tabulation O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bottom-up DP to determine if last index is reachable from first index.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Jump Game problem solved using dynamic programming with tabulation. The intuition is to work backwards from the destination to the source, building up reachability information incrementally. We create a boolean array dp where dp[i] indicates whether index i can reach the last index. We initialize dp[n-1] = True since the last index is trivially reachable from itself. Then we iterate from n-2 down to 0. For each index i, we check all indices j that are reachable from i (i+1 to i+nums[i], bounded by n-1). If any of these j have dp[j] = True, then i can reach the last index through j, so we set dp[i] = True. The final answer is dp[0]. This bottom-up approach avoids recursion overhead and builds solutions systematically. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case when nums[i] is large, as we might check up to n indices for each i. Space complexity is O(n) for the dp array. Connection to L1: This uses the reachability checking subroutine from L1 as its core operation. Common mistakes include: not handling the case where i+nums[i] exceeds array bounds (must use min(i+nums[i], n-1)), initializing all dp values to False except last index, and misunderstanding that we're checking forward jumps from i rather than backward jumps. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): n = len(nums); dp = [False]*n; dp[n-1] = True; for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1); for j in range(i+1, max_jump+1): if dp[j]: dp[i] = True; break; return dp[0]

examples.trace: nums = [2,3,1,1,4], n=5. Initialize dp = [F,F,F,F,T] (index 4 is True). i=3: nums[3]=1, max_jump=min(3+1,4)=4. Check j=4: dp[4]=True → dp[3]=True. i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. Check j=3: dp[3]=True → dp[2]=True. i=1: nums[1]=3, max_jump=min(1+3,4)=4. Check j=2: dp[2]=True → dp[1]=True. i=0: nums[0]=2, max_jump=min(0+2,4)=2. Check j=1: dp[1]=True → dp[0]=True. Final dp=[T,T,T,T,T], return dp[0]=True.

nums = [3,2,1,0,4], n=5. Initialize dp = [F,F,F,F,T]. i=3: nums[3]=0, max_jump=min(3+0,4)=3. Check j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3, so no check → dp[3]=False. i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. Check j=3: dp[3]=False; j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3, so stop → dp[2]=False. i=1: nums[1]=2, max_jump=min(1+2,4)=3. Check j=2: dp[2]=False; j=3: dp[3]=False; j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3 → dp[1]=False. i=0: nums[0]=3, max_jump=min(0+3,4)=3. Check j=1: dp[1]=False; j=2: dp[2]=False; j=3: dp[3]=False; j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3 → dp[0]=False. Final dp=[F,F,F,F,T], return dp[0]=False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking Reachability from Current Index
ladders.desc: Determine if current index can reach any True index within its jump range.
ladders.explanation: This is the core subroutine of the DP solution: given a current index i, the nums array, and the dp array (which marks which indices can reach the end), determine whether i can reach the last index. The intuition is that index i can reach the last index if it can jump to some index j (where j is between i+1 and i+nums[i], inclusive) that already has dp[j] = True. We need to check all possible landing spots from i. The algorithm: 1. Calculate max_reachable = min(i + nums[i], n-1) to avoid index out of bounds. 2. Iterate j from i+1 to max_reachable. 3. If dp[j] is True, return True immediately (since we found a path). 4. If loop completes without finding True, return False. Time complexity is O(k) where k = min(nums[i], n-1-i), which in worst case is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) additional space. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each index i in the main loop of L0. Common mistakes include: forgetting to bound max_reachable by n-1, starting j from i instead of i+1 (you can't jump to yourself as a step), and not breaking early when finding a True value. Real code pattern: def can_reach_from_i(i, nums, dp): n = len(nums); max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1); for j in range(i+1, max_jump+1): if dp[j]: return True; return False

examples.trace: n=5. max_jump = min(1+3,4)=4. Check j=2: dp[2]=False; j=3: dp[3]=False; j=4: dp[4]=True → return True immediately.
n=5. max_jump = min(3+0,4)=3. Check j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3, so no iterations → return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bottom-up DP with Boolean Reachability
ladders.desc: Building dp array backwards where dp[i] depends on dp[i+1...i+nums[i]].
ladders.explanation: This is the central DP technique: solving the problem by building a table (dp array) from the end to the beginning, where each entry dp[i] is computed based on later entries. The intuition is that to know if you can reach the end from index i, you need to know if you can reach the end from indices reachable from i. This creates a dependency chain: dp[i] = OR(dp[j]) for all j where i < j ≤ i+nums[i] and j < n. We start with the base case dp[n-1] = True (the end is reachable from itself). Then for i from n-2 down to 0, we compute dp[i] using the values already computed for j > i. This is bottom-up because we solve smaller subproblems (indices closer to end) first. Time complexity is O(n²) worst-case as each i might check up to n indices. Space complexity is O(n) for dp array. Connection to L1: This logic uses the reachability check from L1 for each i. Common mistakes include: initializing dp all to False (correct), but forgetting to set dp[n-1]=True; iterating i forward instead of backward (would need dp values that aren't computed yet); misunderstanding that dp[i] represents reachability to end from i, not from start. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); dp = [False]*n; dp[n-1] = True; for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1); for j in range(i+1, max_jump+1): if dp[j]: dp[i] = True; break

examples.trace: n=5. Initialize dp=[F,F,F,F,T]. i=3: max_jump=min(3+1,4)=4. Check j=4: dp[4]=T → dp[3]=T. i=2: max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. Check j=3: dp[3]=T → dp[2]=T. i=1: max_jump=min(1+3,4)=4. Check j=2: dp[2]=T → dp[1]=T. i=0: max_jump=min(0+2,4)=2. Check j=1: dp[1]=T → dp[0]=T. Final dp=[T,T,T,T,T].

n=3. Initialize dp=[F,F,T]. i=1: max_jump=min(1+0,2)=1. Check j=2: j=2 > max_jump=1, so no check → dp[1]=F. i=0: max_jump=min(0+1,2)=1. Check j=1: dp[1]=F; j=2: j=2 > max_jump=1 → dp[0]=F. Final dp=[F,F,T].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Backwards with Dependency
ladders.desc: Processing array from end to start where current value depends on later values.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental DP building block: iterating through an array in reverse order while computing values that depend on indices ahead in the array. The intuition is that some problems have natural dependencies where the solution at index i requires knowledge about indices j > i. By iterating backwards, we ensure that when we compute dp[i], all dp[j] for j > i are already computed and available. This is common in bottom-up DP. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an array dp of size n. 2. Set the base case at the end (e.g., dp[n-1] = known value). 3. For i from n-2 down to 0 (inclusive): compute dp[i] using dp[i+1], dp[i+2], ... up to some limit. 4. The answer is typically dp[0]. Time complexity depends on how many later indices each i checks; here O(n²) worst-case. Space complexity O(n). Connection to L2: This is the iteration pattern used in L2's DP logic. Common mistakes include: using forward iteration which would require memoization or recursion; off-by-one errors in loop ranges (e.g., range(n-1, -1, -1) goes from n-1 to 0); not initializing the base case properly. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); dp = [False]*n; dp[n-1] = True; for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): # compute dp[i] using dp[i+1...]

examples.input: n=5, compute dp[i] = (dp[i+1] OR dp[i+2] if within bounds)
examples.trace: Assume dp initially [?,?,?,?,T] with base dp[4]=T. i=3: check dp[4] → dp[3]=T. i=2: check dp[3] and dp[4] → dp[2]=T. i=1: check dp[2], dp[3], dp[4] → dp[1]=T. i=0: check dp[1], dp[2] → dp[0]=T. Final dp=[T,T,T,T,T].

examples.input: n=3, compute dp[i] = dp[i+1] if i+1 < n else False
examples.trace: Assume dp initially [?,?,T] with base dp[2]=T. i=1: check dp[2] → dp[1]=T. i=0: check dp[1] → dp[0]=T. Final dp=[T,T,T].

testCases.input: n=3, base dp[2]=T, compute dp[i]=dp[i+1] OR dp[i+2] if exists
n=3, base dp[2]=F, compute dp[i]=dp[i+1] OR dp[i+2] if exists
n=5, base dp[4]=T, compute dp[i]=dp[i+1] if i%2==0 else dp[i+2]
n=5, base dp[4]=F, compute dp[i]=dp[i+1] if i%2==0 else dp[i+2]
n=6, base dp[5]=T, compute dp[i]=dp[i+1] for i=4 to 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bounded Array Traversal with Min
ladders.desc: Safely accessing array indices within bounds using min() to limit jumps.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation in the Jump Game problem: given an index i and a jump length nums[i], we need to compute the farthest index we can reach from i without going out of the array bounds. The intuition is that if i + nums[i] exceeds the last index (n-1), we can only go up to n-1. We use min(i + nums[i], n-1) to get this safe limit. This ensures all array accesses are valid. The algorithm: 1. Let n = length of array. 2. For index i, compute max_reachable = i + nums[i]. 3. If max_reachable >= n-1, set limit = n-1; else set limit = max_reachable. This is equivalent to limit = min(i + nums[i], n-1). 4. Use this limit to bound loops or conditions. Time complexity O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in L3's iteration to determine how many later indices to check. Common mistakes include: forgetting to use min() and getting index out of bounds; using n instead of n-1 (array indices are 0 to n-1); misunderstanding that nums[i] is jump length, not target index. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); limit = min(i + nums[i], n-1)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Boolean Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Creating and accessing arrays of boolean values using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: using arrays (or lists) to store boolean values (True/False) and accessing them via indices. In programming, an array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory, accessible by an index starting from 0. Boolean arrays are used to represent states (e.g., reachable/not reachable). The intuition is that we can use an array dp where dp[i] stores a True/False value for position i. We initialize it (e.g., all False), set values at specific indices (e.g., dp[0] = True), and read values (e.g., if dp[i]: ...). Key operations: 1. Creation: dp = [False] * n creates an array of n False values. 2. Assignment: dp[index] = value sets the element at index. 3. Access: value = dp[index] retrieves the element. 4. Iteration: for i in range(n): use dp[i]. Time complexity for access is O(1), space O(n). Connection to L4: Boolean arrays are used in L4 to store reachability limits. Common mistakes include: index out of bounds (indices must be 0 to n-1); confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based; not initializing array properly. Real code pattern: n = 5; dp = [False]*n; dp[0] = True; if dp[0]: print('yes')

examples.input: Create boolean array of size 3, set index 2 to True, read index 1
examples.trace: dp = [False]*3 → [F,F,F]. dp[2] = True → [F,F,T]. dp[1] is False.

examples.input: Create boolean array of size 1, set index 0 to True
examples.trace: dp = [False]*1 → [F]. dp[0] = True → [T].

testCases.input: size=4, set indices 0 and 3 to True
size=5, set all indices to True
size=2, set index 0 to True, index 1 to False
size=6, set even indices to True
size=3, set index 2 to True, read index 2
size=4, set index 3 to True, read index 0

approaches.name: Greedy Backward Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Start from the last index and move backward, updating the target to the current index if it is reachable. This checks if the start can reach the end by tracking the minimal reachable point.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with Greedy Backward Scan
ladders.desc: Determine if you can reach the last index starting from first index using backward greedy approach.
ladders.explanation: The Jump Game problem asks whether you can reach the last index of an array starting from the first index, where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The greedy backward scan approach works by starting from the last index and moving backward, updating our target to the current index whenever we find a position that can reach the current target. This approach is optimal because if we can reach the last index from some position, then reaching that position becomes our new goal. The intuition is that we're working backward to find the minimal reachable point - if we can eventually work our way back to index 0, then the entire path is reachable. The algorithm initializes target as the last index, then iterates from second-last to first index. For each position i, if i + nums[i] >= target (meaning position i can reach the current target), we update target = i. After the loop, if target == 0, we can reach the end. This approach is O(n) time and O(1) space. Common mistakes include incorrect inequality (should be >= not >), forgetting to update target properly, or misunderstanding that nums[i] represents maximum jump, not exact jump. This connects to L1 as the complete implementation of the backward scan algorithm.

examples.trace:
nums = [2,3,1,1,4], n=5
1. Initialize target = 4 (last index)
2. i=3: nums[3]=1, 3+1=4 >= target(4) → target=3
3. i=2: nums[2]=1, 2+1=3 >= target(3) → target=2
4. i=1: nums[1]=3, 1+3=4 >= target(2) → target=1
5. i=0: nums[0]=2, 0+2=2 >= target(1) → target=0
6. After loop: target=0 → return true

nums = [3,2,1,0,4], n=5
1. Initialize target = 4 (last index)
2. i=3: nums[3]=0, 3+0=3 < target(4) → target stays 4
3. i=2: nums[2]=1, 2+1=3 < target(4) → target stays 4
4. i=1: nums[1]=2, 1+2=3 < target(4) → target stays 4
5. i=0: nums[0]=3, 0+3=3 < target(4) → target stays 4
6. After loop: target=4 ≠ 0 → return false

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backward Reachability Check
ladders.desc: Check if current position can reach target position and update target accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This is the core subroutine of the greedy backward scan approach. The function takes a current index i, the nums array, and a target index, then determines if position i can reach the current target. The condition is: if i + nums[i] >= target, then position i can reach the target (since nums[i] is the maximum jump length from i). When this condition is true, we update the target to i, making i our new goal to reach from earlier positions. The intuition is that we're finding the earliest position from which we can reach our current target - by moving this target backward through the array, we eventually check if index 0 can reach the final target. This subroutine runs in O(1) time and uses O(1) space. Common mistakes include using > instead of >= (if i can jump exactly to target, it's reachable), or not understanding that nums[i] represents maximum jump, not required jump. This connects to L0 as the main loop that repeatedly calls this subroutine, and to L2 which focuses on the reachability condition itself.

examples.trace:
nums[3] = 1
Check: i + nums[i] = 3 + 1 = 4
Compare: 4 >= target(4) → true
Therefore position 3 can reach target 4
Update target to 3

nums[2] = 1
Check: i + nums[i] = 2 + 1 = 3
Compare: 3 >= target(4) → false
Therefore position 2 cannot reach target 4
Target remains 4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Reachability Condition i + nums[i] >= target
ladders.desc: The mathematical condition that determines if position i can reach target position.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental inequality that drives the greedy backward scan algorithm: i + nums[i] >= target. Here, i is the current position index, nums[i] is the maximum jump length from that position, and target is the index we're trying to reach. The condition checks whether the maximum reach from position i (i + nums[i]) is at least the target index. If true, position i can reach the target (since we can jump up to nums[i] steps forward). The intuition is simple: from position i, you can jump to any position from i+1 to i+nums[i] inclusive. So if target falls within this range, it's reachable. This is an O(1) check. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the inequality direction, forgetting that it's >= (not >) because reaching exactly the target is sufficient, or confusing nums[i] with required jump rather than maximum jump. This connects to L1 as the condition used in the subroutine, and to L3 which covers array indexing fundamentals.

examples.trace:
Calculate maximum reach: i + nums[i] = 2 + 3 = 5
Check if 5 >= 4: true
Interpretation: From position 2 with max jump 3, you can reach positions 3, 4, or 5
Since target 4 is within this range, it's reachable

Calculate maximum reach: i + nums[i] = 1 + 0 = 1
Check if 1 >= 2: false
Interpretation: From position 1 with max jump 0, you can only stay at position 1
Target 2 is not reachable

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Jump Range Calculation
ladders.desc: Calculate the range of positions reachable from a given array index with a given jump length.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves understanding how array indexing works and calculating the reachable range from a position. Given an array index i and a jump length j (where j = nums[i]), the reachable positions are all indices from i+1 to i+j inclusive. This is because from position i, you can jump 1, 2, ..., j steps forward. The maximum reach is i + j. This calculation is fundamental because the Jump Game problem is all about whether target indices fall within these reachable ranges. The intuition is visualizing positions on a number line: from point i, with jump capacity j, you can land anywhere up to j steps ahead. This operation is O(1) time and space. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (thinking reachable positions are i to i+j instead of i+1 to i+j), or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. This connects to L2 as it provides the basis for the reachability condition, and to L4 which covers basic arithmetic operations.

examples.trace:
Starting position: index 2
Jump length: 3
Minimum reach: i+1 = 2+1 = 3
Maximum reach: i+jump = 2+3 = 5
Reachable positions: all integers from 3 to 5 inclusive: 3, 4, 5

Starting position: index 0
Jump length: 0
Minimum reach: i+1 = 0+1 = 1
Maximum reach: i+jump = 0+0 = 0
Since maximum reach (0) < minimum reach (1), no positions are reachable
You cannot jump from position 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access and Integer Addition
ladders.desc: Access an array element and add it to an index.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic operation needed for the Jump Game solution: accessing an array element nums[i] and adding it to the index i. In programming terms, this involves: 1) Array indexing: retrieving the value at position i using nums[i], and 2) Integer addition: computing i + nums[i]. This operation is O(1) time and O(1) space. The intuition is straightforward: we need to know both our current position (i) and how far we can jump from there (nums[i]) to determine where we can reach. Common mistakes include index out of bounds errors (accessing beyond array length), confusing array indices with values, or incorrect order of operations. This connects to L3 as it's used to calculate reachable ranges, and to L5 which covers basic array concepts.

examples.trace:
1. Access nums[1]: nums[1] = 3
2. Add i to nums[i]: 1 + 3 = 4
3. Result: 4
Interpretation: From position 1, with max jump 3, maximum reach is position 4

1. Access nums[2]: nums[2] = 2
2. Add i to nums[i]: 2 + 2 = 4
3. Result: 4
Interpretation: From position 2, with max jump 2, maximum reach is position 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Zero-based Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as collections of elements accessible by numerical indices starting at 0.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by a numerical index. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. For an array of length n, valid indices are 0 through n-1. This concept is crucial for the Jump Game because positions in the game correspond to array indices, and jump lengths are stored as array values. The intuition is simple: think of an array as a numbered list where position numbers start from 0. Accessing an element is O(1) time. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, accessing beyond array bounds (index >= length), or confusing indices with values. This connects to L4 as it provides the foundation for array element access.

examples.trace:
Array length: 4
Index 0 → value 10
Index 1 → value 20
Index 2 → value 30
Index 3 → value 40
Invalid indices: -1, 4, 5, etc.
To access value at index 2: array[2] = 30

Array length: 3
Valid indices: 0, 1, 2
Index 0 → value 5
Index 1 → value 3
Index 2 → value 7
Last index = length - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2
Value at last index: array[2] = 7
